title: St. Cyril of Alexandria, Archangel Michael, St. Basil of Ancyra & St. Drosida of Antioch
narrative:
title": "St. Cyril of Alexandria, Archangel Michael, St. Basil of Ancyra & St. Drosida of Antioch

narrative": "St. Cyril of Alexandria, the Archangel Michael, St. Basil of Ancyra the Confessor, and St. Drosida of Antioch

On the twenty-second of Megabit, the holy Church brings together the great Alexandrian theologian of the Incarnation, the captain of the heavenly hosts, a confessor bishop of Asia Minor, and a martyr of Antioch who was the daughter of an emperor — four witnesses whose lives demonstrate that the confession of faith is costly at every social level, from the highest to the most ordinary. Saint Cyril of Alexandria dominates this day's assembly with the weight of his theological achievement: the man who secured the Church's confession of the Theotokos at Ephesus, who formulated the Miaphysite Christology in its most precise and enduring form, and whose twelve anathemas against Nestorius remain the most exact instruments of Christological definition in the Oriental tradition. His methods in the Nestorian controversy were not always gentle, and the tradition does not suppress this; but his theology was, and his legacy is received by the Ethiopian church as the foundation on which its own confession of Christ rests.

The Archangel Michael's second appearance this month falls on the twenty-second day, which in some regional Ethiopian traditions carries an additional Marian association — the two great intercessors, Michael and Mary, are sometimes commemorated together in the Ethiopian devotional calendar, their monthly feasts falling on adjacent days. The Megabit commemoration of Michael follows immediately upon Cyril's, and the juxtaposition is theologically suggestive: the one who defined with philosophical precision how the divine and human are united in Christ stands beside the one who executes the divine will with the swift immediacy of pure creaturely responsiveness. The theology and the angelic service are two expressions of the same adoration.

Saint Basil of Ancyra is this day's figure of confessor-bishop: a bishop of the Cappadocian city who was deposed and exiled multiple times during the Arian controversy, not for the Nicene faith precisely — he was for a period associated with the homoiousian position — but for his consistent resistance to the imperial imposition of theological solutions by authority rather than by argument and persuasion. His is a nuanced story that the tradition preserves without simplification: a bishop who was not always perfectly orthodox by the Nicene standard but who maintained throughout his life the principle that the faith must be freely confessed rather than compelled, and who suffered for that principle as surely as for the Nicene formula itself.

Saint Drosida of Antioch is this day's discovery: the daughter of the Emperor Trajan who was converted to Christianity in the early second century and who, with four of her companions, was arrested for going by night to the bodies of Christian martyrs to give them proper burial. She was brought before her own father for interrogation — one of the most painful of all martyrological situations — and was condemned and executed. The tradition notes that Trajan was genuinely astonished by his daughter's faith and genuinely unable to compromise his imperial role even for his own child, and that this astonishment was not without its own form of testimony: the emperor who could not bend his daughter's faith, however much he tried, was himself witnessing, however unwillingly, to the power that his daughter had found. Amen.
